evidence_quotes.quote: Free to Play vs. Pay to Progress: Regulators need to know that this mechanism is the foundation of a huge real-world economy, and that clumsy regulation here can easily destroy the mobile games industry throughout the EU.
The choice to spend to progress faster in a game is, by its very nature, self-regulated.
Users have the clear choice to accelerate their progress in a game (and pay the business for the product they are enjoying) or not.
There is already a powerful dynamic in place that drives game design teams to balance the pace of progress for F2P vs P2P - the need to keep people playing their games.
This dynamic forces games to meet player's thresholds for progress in games, regardless of if they spend real world money or not.
That said, I'd urge regulators to resist the urge entirely to meddle with this effective market dynamic.

paragraphs.text: Free to Play vs. Pay to Progress: Regulators need to know that this mechanism is the foundation of a huge real-world economy, and that clumsy regulation here can easily destroy the mobile games industry throughout the EU. Further, the choice to spend to progress faster in a game is, by its very nature, self-regulated. Users have the clear choice to accelerate their progress in a game (and pay the business for the product they are enjoying) or not. They can also leave the game if they aren't enjoying the experience. There is already a powerful dynamic in place that drives game design teams to balance the pace of progress for F2P vs P2P - the need to keep people playing their games. This dynamic forces games to meet player's thresholds for progress in games, regardless of if they spend real world money or not. Games either get this ballence right, or perish - to use a common expression "Darwin sorts it out". That said, I'd urge regulators to resist the urge entirely to meddle with this effective market dynamic. Thank you.
